title: New Fans

jokes.text: Bret suggests putting hay in your mouth as a cool image choice, demonstrating enthusiastically with 'Hey, how's it going? Hey, man.'
jokes.setup: The band is brainstorming ways to look cooler — leather jacket, cigarette behind ear — normal rock band image ideas.
jokes.punchline: Bret's contribution is hay hanging from the mouth, delivered as a genuine suggestion, with a full sincere demonstration.
jokes.explanation: The escalating absurdity of the image-improvement list lands well because the hay suggestion is delivered with complete sincerity. The demonstration ('Hey, how's it going?') compounds the absurdity without overselling. Pure Bret — earnest, odd, and specific enough to feel character-driven rather than random.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Where's yours?

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: It's on my chair.

jokes.text: 'Cause there was probably only one entry. — No, there was hundreds, actually. — Really? — Yep. They're all from Mel.
jokes.setup: Murray announces they have a winner for the fan competition. Jemaine immediately guesses it's Mel.
jokes.punchline: Murray confirms it's 'hundreds' of entries — all from Mel — subverting the expectation that Jemaine was wrong about only one entry.
jokes.explanation: Beautifully structured two-beat joke. The first beat is Jemaine correctly predicting one entry and being 'wrong' — the punchline recontextualizes 'hundreds' as both confirming and exceeding his low expectation. Tight economy, reveals Mel's obsessive character efficiently. The structure earns a high score.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: you could have a bit of hay coming out of your mouth... a little bit of hay.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: All the time?

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Yeah.

jokes.text: And one from Jemaine. — Right. — But he was disqualified.
jokes.setup: We've established all entries came from Mel. Murray casually mentions Jemaine also entered.
jokes.punchline: Jemaine was disqualified — he entered his own fan competition.
jokes.explanation: The detail that Jemaine entered his own fan competition — and was disqualified as a result — does efficient double character work: it reveals Jemaine's vanity/neediness while also revealing the band's desperate situation. Compact and earned.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: 'Cause there was probably only one entry.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: No, there was hundreds, actually.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Really?

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Yep. They're all from Mel.

jokes.text: It's not a good look. You don't see Bret on the fan list. — Yeah, well, that's 'cause I'm not a fan of the band. I'm more a fan of popular bands like the Bee Gees, Pearl Jam.
jokes.setup: Murray says it's bad optics for a band member to be on their own fan list.
jokes.punchline: Bret clarifies he's not on the list because he genuinely isn't a fan — he prefers the Bee Gees and Pearl Jam.
jokes.explanation: Bret's deadpan admission that he prefers 'popular bands' to his own band is a perfect encapsulation of the show's self-deprecating worldview. The specific choices of Bee Gees and Pearl Jam — one campy, one grunge — make it funnier than a vague reference would. The joke does deep character work while being brief.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: And one from Jemaine.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Right.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: But he was disqualified.

jokes.text: World Music Jam? We don't play world music. Do we? — Yes. Where's New Zealand from if it's not from the world?
jokes.setup: Bret is confused about being booked for a world music night.
jokes.punchline: Murray defends it with perfectly circular logic: New Zealand is from the world, therefore they play world music.
jokes.explanation: This is exceptional. Murray's logic is technically unassailable — all music is from the world — making it simultaneously absurd and impossible to argue against. It's a perfect distillation of Murray's managerial incompetence masked as confident authority. The economy is perfect: one line does it all. Comparable in structure to the 8.0 anchor — a sharp, unexpected, highly quotable, perfectly economical line that only Murray could deliver.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Because you can't be a fan of the band.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Why?

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: It's not a good look.

jokes.text: The 'Ladies of the World' song — escalating list of nationalities/descriptors culminating in 'Amphibian / Lady' and 'Hermaphrodite / Lady-man lady'
jokes.setup: An R&B-pastiche love song celebrating all the ladies of the world, running through a call-and-response list of nationalities and types.
jokes.punchline: The list of romantic female descriptors escalates from geographic (Caribbean, Parisian, Bolivian) through the religious (Presbyterian) to the biological (Hermaphrodite), each treated with equal reverence.
jokes.explanation: The escalation is perfectly calibrated. 'Presbyterian / Lady' is already a gear-shift into absurdity, but 'Hermaphrodite / Lady-man lady' delivered with the exact same reverent musical treatment as 'Caribbean Lady' is the peak. The song commits completely to its inclusive thesis, which makes each new category funnier. The follow-through into 'your sexy lady bits and your sexy man bits too' is genuinely inventive comedy writing.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Yeah, well, that's 'cause I'm not a fan of the band. I'm more a fan of popular bands like the Bee Gees, Pearl Jam.

jokes.text: Redheads not warheads / Blondes not bombs / We're talking about brunettes not fighter jets
jokes.setup: The song has been arguing that world peace could be achieved through love-making. It moves into a slogan section.
jokes.punchline: The peace-advocacy slogans follow a pattern (hair color / weapon rhyme) that works twice then breaks down with 'brunettes not fighter jets' — which doesn't scan the same way but is delivered with equal confidence.
jokes.explanation: 'Brunettes not fighter jets' is a lovely bit of internal song comedy — the pattern breaks in a way that feels like the songwriter ran out of good rhymes and just committed anyway. The specificity of 'fighter jets' over the simpler rhyme makes it weirder and funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: First prize... the chance to cook for you two tomorrow night.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: That's a terrible prize. I'm glad I didn't win that.

jokes.text: Where every lady gets a little piece of Bretty
jokes.setup: The song has been building a grand utopian vision of world peace through universal love.
jokes.punchline: The sweeping vision collapses into the specific and self-serving: every lady gets 'a little piece of Bretty' — with the diminutive 'Bretty' undercutting any grandiosity.
jokes.explanation: This is exceptional writing. The bathos of deflating a John Lennon-esque utopian vision into 'a little piece of Bretty' is perfect. 'Bretty' specifically — the -y suffix making it both self-aggrandizing and pathetically small — is a masterstroke. This could only be Bret's song. High craft score is justified: it's original, structurally tight, deeply character-specific, economical, and completely earned by the song's own internal logic.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: I've already e-mailed her pretending to be you saying you're excited.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Were you pretending to be me as well?

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Yes, that's what I do. It's... it's easier.

jokes.text: I've been to Paris, Wellington and Amsterdam / And a wham-bam, merci, danke, thank you, ma'am / I don't care if you're ugly or you're skanky or you're small
jokes.setup: The song is establishing Bret's romantic credentials and inclusive attitude.
jokes.punchline: 'Wellington' sandwiched between Paris and Amsterdam as an equivalent romantic destination, followed by 'ugly or skanky or small' as the song's inclusive quality-floor.
jokes.explanation: Wellington's inclusion among European romantic capitals is classic Conchords self-deprecating New Zealand humor. 'Ugly or skanky or small' as the song's advertised inclusivity is a great tonal collapse — the bar for inclusion is extremely low, which makes the altruism hollow and funny.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Actually, next time you see Mel, you might want to apologize for your last e-mail.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Okay. Why?

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: It's just, I think she was quite insulted.

jokes.text: The Flute of the Commodores. Come on up, guys.
jokes.setup: The MC is introducing Flight of the Conchords.
jokes.punchline: He gets their name completely wrong — 'The Flute of the Commodores' — a mangling that combines two unrelated musical references.
jokes.explanation: A reliable joke in the show's universe — the band is so obscure their name is routinely mangled. 'The Flute of the Commodores' is a particularly good mangle because it sounds plausible as a world music act name, which makes it funnier than pure nonsense.

dialogue.speaker: MC
dialogue.line: Bonjour, lay ho, salaam, nabadeey, konnichiwa, and hola. Welcome to Tuesday World Music Jam.

jokes.text: Really good. Different start to the song. — What were you doing there? — No, it's the same. — Oh, okay. So... [hums the tuning sequence] — Well, we were tuning. — Oh, okay.
jokes.setup: Murray compliments their set and asks about the 'different start to the song.'
jokes.punchline: What Murray thought was a creative intro was them tuning. He accepts the explanation with 'Oh, okay' — suggesting he genuinely couldn't tell the difference.
jokes.explanation: The payoff to the tuning gag. Murray's inability to distinguish tuning from music is both a character joke (incompetent manager) and a band joke (their music is indistinguishable from tuning). The double target makes it richer.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: World Music Jam? We don't play world music. Do we?

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Yes. Where's New Zealand from if it's not from the world?

jokes.text: I bought you guys a beer. — I don't even drink beer. — I don't drink it either. — Just drink it, will you? It's good for the rock 'n' roll image.
jokes.setup: Murray presents beers to the band after their set.
jokes.punchline: Neither band member drinks beer. Murray insists anyway for image reasons.
jokes.explanation: Solid character beat. The image-management theme running through the episode is reinforced. Murray forcing non-drinkers to drink beer 'for image' is the show in miniature — trying to be rock stars when they fundamentally aren't.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: There's a lot of hot women here.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Yeah.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Look at those two.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Whoa. Whew.

jokes.text: I do things for my image... make it more rock 'n' roll with the goatee and everything. — I don't like this goatee. I hate it. — Makes you look like a goat, doesn't it? — Yeah, exactly. But you've gotta make sacrifices for success.
jokes.setup: Murray uses his own goatee as an example of image sacrifice.
jokes.punchline: The goatee makes him look like a goat — something he is aware of and accepts as a necessary sacrifice.
jokes.explanation: Murray's goatee self-awareness is funny — he knows it looks bad, frames it as noble sacrifice, and moves on. The flat acknowledgment 'yeah, exactly' followed by the pivot to 'sacrifices for success' is well-timed deadpan.

dialogue.line: Ladies song with increasingly absurd nationality descriptors culminating in 'Amphibian Lady' and 'Presbyterian Lady'

jokes.text: Bret forcing himself to drink the beer — reaction shot implied — thinking of something happy, visible grimacing.
jokes.setup: Murray coaches Bret through drinking beer he finds disgusting for image purposes.
jokes.punchline: Murray instructs 'Think about something happy' while Bret apparently struggles through it. Then Murray declares 'You look cool!'
jokes.explanation: The 'think about something happy' coaching for drinking beer is a genuinely funny misdirection of therapy-speak. Visual payoff of Bret looking cool while internally suffering is classic Conchords performance comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Hermaphrodite Lady-man lady / All you sexy hermaphrodite lady-man ladies

jokes.text: Hey, Jemaine, those girls are looking over at us. — Do you think they might be looking at me? — They're looking at all of us!
jokes.setup: Bret notices girls looking at them.
jokes.punchline: Jemaine immediately assumes it's specifically about him, requiring Bret's correction.
jokes.explanation: A quick Jemaine vanity beat. Works but is fairly standard sitcom character-revelation humor. Functions better in context as part of the escalating image/vanity theme.

dialogue.line: If every soldier in the world / Put down his weapon and picked up a woman / What a peaceful world this world would be

jokes.text: Tell you what, maybe if I pretend to go to the toilet, you guys see if they're looking at me. [Murray watches] — No, they're not looking at me! — I might actually go to the toilet now that I'm over here!
jokes.setup: Jemaine devises a scheme to test if the girls are watching him specifically.
jokes.punchline: The girls are not looking at him. He announces this from the toilet area, defeated, and then converts the pointless trip into a functional one.
jokes.explanation: The pivot — 'I might actually go to the toilet now that I'm over here' — is delightful. Converting the failed vanity mission into practical errand is beautifully anticlimactic. Jemaine shouting his defeat from across the room is a strong visual/audio comedy beat.

dialogue.line: Redheads not warheads / Blondes not bombs / We're talking about brunettes not fighter jets

jokes.text: For the dinner that I won, I was wondering if you liked arugula or romaine lettuce.
jokes.setup: The group is at a crowded venue, new fans are approaching, and Jemaine has just returned from his toilet-vanity mission.
jokes.punchline: Mel cuts in to ask about salad preferences for a dinner still days away — completely oblivious to the social situation.
jokes.explanation: Mel's interruption with mundane dinner logistics at exactly the wrong moment is perfect character work. Her total unawareness of the dynamic she's disrupting (the guys meeting new fans) is both funny and slightly sad.

dialogue.line: Sweet 16s not M-16s

jokes.text: I really like that one you did about how you liked to rock the party. — I like to rock the party. — You must have been really wasted when you wrote that.
jokes.setup: A new fan compliments their song — 'Who likes to rock the party' — which was only three lines and cut off.
jokes.punchline: The fan assumes the song's simplicity indicates they were drugged when writing it.
jokes.explanation: A great callback to their truncated performance. The fan's logic is perversely correct — a song that's just 'I like to rock the party' repeated does sound like it was written high. Jemaine's likely awkward response to having their laziness/incompetence mistaken for artistic transgression is implied.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Where every lady gets a little piece of Bretty

jokes.text: Oh, yes, we were tripping when we wrote that one, that's right. — We were totally whacked off. — What? — Whacked off? — Uh, that's just a drug term we use back in New Zealand when we're out of it. We say 'Oh, man, I was whacked off last night.'
jokes.setup: The guys have agreed with the fan's assumption that they were on drugs. Bret adds 'whacked off' as a drug term.
jokes.punchline: 'Whacked off' is emphatically not a drug term — it's a masturbation euphemism. The guys are oblivious to this and double down with a usage example.
jokes.explanation: The New Zealanders-innocently-using-American-slang-wrong is a recurring show theme executed well here. The girls' 'What?' registers the double meaning the guys don't catch. Bret's confident elaboration — 'Oh, man, I was whacked off last night' as an example sentence — escalates it perfectly.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: I don't care if you're ugly or you're skanky or you're small / Just want to do a little something special for you all

jokes.text: Hey, do you want to join the fan list? Here we are. Here's a card with our website on. And you can get online and e-mail the guys and they'll reply to you.
jokes.setup: The girls are clearly interested in the band as people/potential dates.
jokes.punchline: Murray materializes to recruit them to the fan list and advertises the ability to email the guys — which he answers himself while pretending to be them.
jokes.explanation: Murray's e-mail promise is a callback joke — the audience knows he'll respond as both of them. The girls don't know this, creating dramatic irony. His enthusiasm for the process is a good character beat.

dialogue.speaker: MC
dialogue.line: Something completely different here. They're from New Zealand. We have... The Flute of the Commodores.

jokes.text: Well, how about that, eh? A real rock 'n' roll band. — That'll be the beer, you see? — Yeah. — My goatee probably would've helped as well. — I think it was more the beer. — They weren't looking at your goatee. — Yes they were. One of them was.
jokes.setup: The guys reflect on the new fans they've attracted.
jokes.punchline: Jemaine insists one of the girls was specifically looking at his goatee. The specificity of 'one of them was' against Murray and Bret's unified skepticism is the joke.
jokes.explanation: Jemaine's specific claim ('one of them was') is a good character beat — the specificity implies he's watching carefully enough to have a theory, while the claim itself is almost certainly wrong. Callbacks the goatee image thread effectively.

dialogue.line: Song opening with extensive tuning: A, B... A... D, G, G, G... G, G, G... G...

jokes.text: I got a shirt that's got a mouse having sex with another mouse in a mousetrap.
jokes.setup: Bret asks Dave for something cool but also sexy to wear.
jokes.punchline: Dave immediately produces a shirt depicting mice copulating in a mousetrap, which he considers a guaranteed success with women.
jokes.explanation: This is exceptional. The specificity — not just a dirty shirt, but mice, specifically, in a mousetrap — is the joke. It's the kind of image only Dave would own, only Dave would wear, and only Dave would describe as 'I score chicks in this shirt all the time.' The mousetrap element adds a dark/painful dimension that makes the sexual image even more absurd. Fully earns the high craft score.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Who likes to rock the party?

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: I like to rock the party

jokes.text: I guarantee you, I was just pumping away in this thing the other night.
jokes.setup: Dave is showing them the mouse-sex shirt as a successful seduction garment.
jokes.punchline: 'Pumping away' — Dave's chosen expression for sex while holding a shirt depicting mice copulating.
jokes.explanation: Works as a Dave character beat — his crudeness mirrors the shirt's imagery. Functional extension of the previous joke rather than a standalone.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Different start to the song.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: What were you doing there?

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: No, it's the same.

jokes.text: I was hoping to dress something like Prince. — Kind of erogenous, huh? — Yeah, but toned down a bit, like, Prince if he was just going to the zoo or the supermarket. — Casual Prince? — Casual Prince.
jokes.setup: Bret explains his fashion aspiration.
jokes.punchline: The concept of 'Casual Prince' — Prince at the grocery store, toned down — is both specific and absurd. Dave immediately labels it, treating it as a legitimate aesthetic category.
jokes.explanation: 'Casual Prince' is a brilliant two-word comedy concept. The zoo/supermarket specificity grounds the absurdity. It's highly quotable because it immediately conjures a perfect image while being impossible and funny. Dave's instantaneous adoption of the term as a real category ('Casual Prince?') validates the concept comedically. This is close to the 8.0 anchor level — unexpected, quotable, economical.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: I bought you guys a beer.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: I don't even drink beer.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: I don't drink it either.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Just drink it, will you?

jokes.text: [Shh!] [pause] — Jesus! — Sorry guys. My roommate's being kind of a jerk.
jokes.setup: Dave is rooting through his closet for the Prince blouse.
jokes.punchline: Something (implied: a person or noise) startles everyone. Dave dismisses it as his 'roommate being a jerk' — setting up the reveal that follows.
jokes.explanation: Setup beat for the parents joke that follows. Works as tension-builder but the comedy is deferred to the next exchange.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: I do things for my image... make it more rock 'n' roll with the goatee and everything. I don't like this goatee. I hate it.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Makes you look like a goat, doesn't it?

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Yeah, exactly.

jokes.text: She thinks she's my mom. — That is your mom. — Dude, you think I live with my parents? — Oh, is that your dad, though? — No. That's just some old, crotchety Indian couple that I sublet to. The weird thing is they look like me.
jokes.setup: Dave denies living with his parents.
jokes.punchline: He claims to sublet to an old Indian couple who coincidentally look like him — and has an elaborate non-explanation for why they have photos of young Dave.
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalating denial joke. Dave's insistence deepens with each beat: denying parents → claiming subletters → acknowledging resemblance → explaining away the childhood photos as computer-generated. The straight-faced commitment to an increasingly untenable position, and Bret's matter-of-fact responses, makes this a strong piece of character comedy. 'They make them on the computer' as the final explanation earns the high score — it's both absurd and reveals Dave's psychological investment in the denial.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Tell you what, maybe if I pretend to go to the toilet, you guys see if they're looking at me.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Okay?

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Yep.

jokes.text: Actually, Jemaine, I've had a really good response from something that happened with you when you were alone with the webcam. — Do you know what that would be? — I didn't know we had a webcam.
jokes.setup: Murray reveals the bedroom webcam has been broadcasting. He mentions a particular fan-popular moment involving Jemaine alone.
jokes.punchline: The implication is clear (Jemaine was masturbating on camera). Jemaine's defense is simply that he didn't know the webcam existed — true, but not exculpatory given the audience.
jokes.explanation: The joke is entirely implicit — Murray never says it, Jemaine never confirms it, and the audience fills it in completely. 'Do you know what that would be?' asked with Murray's gentle management tone is perfect. 'I didn't know we had a webcam' as the repeated defense (stated twice in close succession) suggests Jemaine is working through the implications in real time. Exceptional comedic restraint.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: No, they're not looking at me! I might actually go to the toilet now that I'm over here!

jokes.text: I think I might sleep in the lounge from now on. — Oh, okay. Well, here we go. There's the lounge. [shows webcam of the lounge too]
jokes.setup: Jemaine decides to sleep in the lounge for privacy after the bedroom webcam revelation.
jokes.punchline: Murray immediately shows him the lounge is also on webcam.
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation. The setup-to-punchline ratio is exquisite — Jemaine has one sentence of solution before it's immediately destroyed. 'Oh, okay. Well, here we go' suggests Murray considers this totally normal to show, which is funnier than any defensive reaction. The joke is completely structural — every word earns its place.

dialogue.speaker: Fan
dialogue.line: You must have been really wasted when you wrote that.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: No.

dialogue.speaker: Fan
dialogue.line: Do you mean on drugs?

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Oh, yes, we were tripping when we wrote that one, that's right.

jokes.text: Anyway, they grew big beards, their old fans didn't like it. The new fans didn't like them without the beards, and then they had a do-we-have-a-beard-or-not situation.
jokes.setup: Murray is telling the ZZ Top cautionary tale as career advice.
jokes.punchline: Murray summarizes ZZ Top's entire career arc as a 'do-we-have-a-beard-or-not situation' — treating this as a recognized industry phenomenon.
jokes.explanation: 'Do-we-have-a-beard-or-not situation' is a great Murray coinage — he hyphenates it as if it's a known industry term. The invented music history is confidently wrong in a very specific way. High character integration score because this is quintessential Murray: treating invented expertise as real wisdom.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: We were totally whacked off.

dialogue.speaker: Fan
dialogue.line: What?

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Whacked off?

dialogue.speaker: Fan
dialogue.line: Uh, that's just a drug term we use back in New Zealand

jokes.text: Is this relevant? — Yes!
jokes.setup: Murray has just explained the ZZ Top beard situation at length.
jokes.punchline: Jemaine questions relevance. Murray confirms it with complete confidence.
jokes.explanation: A quick beat that earns its economy. Murray's 'Yes!' is funnier for how emphatically he believes the ZZ Top beard situation is applicable. The exchange is only two lines but lands well as a button on the ZZ Top sequence.

dialogue.speaker: Dave
dialogue.line: I got a shirt that's got a mouse having sex with another mouse in a mousetrap. I score chicks in this shirt all the time.

jokes.text: What was your name again? — It's Rain. — Oh. That's nice, like... kind of like bad weather.
jokes.setup: At the dinner, Murray is getting acquainted with the new fan named Rain.
jokes.punchline: Murray's attempt at a compliment about her name is immediately undercut by his own follow-through — 'like bad weather.'
jokes.explanation: Murray's accidental social incompetence — he starts saying her name is nice, then adds 'like bad weather' as though that's a positive qualifier — is a reliable well of Murray character humor. The pause after 'that's nice' while he searches for the connection is implied in the transcript.

dialogue.speaker: Dave
dialogue.line: I guarantee you, I was just pumping away in this thing the other night.

jokes.text: I check it regularly. Do you check it regularly? — No. — No. — Do you not have a computer, or... — No, I do have a computer. — Oh, you can't read.
jokes.setup: Murray asks the new fans if they check the fan website regularly.
jokes.punchline: When they say no, Murray runs through possible explanations — no computer — and when that's denied, immediately jumps to illiteracy.
jokes.explanation: The logic leap from 'has a computer, doesn't check website' to 'can't read' is perfect Murray. He genuinely cannot conceive of someone choosing not to engage with the fan site, so the only explanations are infrastructure or literacy. 'Oh, you can't read' delivered as a sympathetic realization rather than an insult is a strong character beat.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: I was hoping to dress something like Prince. Kind of erogenous, huh?

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Yeah, but toned down a bit, like, Prince if he was just going to the zoo or the supermarket.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Casual Prince?

jokes.text: Mel's backstory: college professor husband she pursued, restraining orders, 'love overcame... I always get what I want.'
jokes.setup: A new fan asks about the older man in the photo — Mel's husband Doug.
jokes.punchline: Mel's romantic love story involves restraining orders from the professor and his family before 'love overcame' — she reframes stalking as romance.
jokes.explanation: This is among the episode's best joke sequences. Mel's account is delivered with the warmth of a romantic anecdote while describing what is clearly a stalking situation. 'Love overcame' as the euphemism for 'she wore him down until the legal obstacles lapsed' is chilling and funny. 'I always get what I want' as the romantic coda, directed at the band members present, is genuinely threatening. The joke does substantial character work: Mel's fixation on the band is now understood as part of a pattern.

dialogue.speaker: Dave
dialogue.line: Jesus! Sorry guys. My roommate's... being kind of a jerk.

jokes.text: But you don't drink beer, Bret. — I drink it all the time. — I could drink a whole glass of beer. — I thought it always made you go to the toilet.
jokes.setup: Bret, wanting to escape the dinner table and get to the new fans, requests beer.
jokes.punchline: Mel reveals she knows intimate details about Bret's beer tolerance — that it sends him immediately to the toilet — exposing both his lie and her surveillance.
jokes.explanation: Mel's knowledge of Bret's beer physiology is both funny (she knows this because she obsessively monitors him) and structurally elegant (it sets up his escape plan perfectly). The callback to the image/beer thread is well-placed. 'I could drink a whole glass of beer' as Bret's overclaim is funny on its own.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Is that a woman's blouse?

dialogue.speaker: Dave
dialogue.line: No.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Looks like a blouse.

dialogue.speaker: Dave
dialogue.line: No no no. This is what you're looking for, man. That's totally Prince.

jokes.text: Hey, guys, watch this. [extended pause — Bret watches for his moment to escape] — Great. — Thanks, Mel. — There's your beer. — Yum! Yum! That's good. Wow. — I just need to go to the toilet. Excuse me.
jokes.setup: Bret's plan is to use beer to get to the toilet and make contact with Summer and Rain. He signals Jemaine to watch his scheme.
jokes.punchline: Bret forces himself through the beer — 'Yum! Yum! That's good. Wow.' — with comically excessive performance, then immediately deploys the toilet excuse.
jokes.explanation: The plan is threadbare and everyone (audience included) can see through it. The 'Yum! Yum! That's good. Wow.' performance is visibly strained. The payoff of immediately excusing himself to the toilet — exactly as Mel predicted — executes the callback with good timing. Visual comedy inferred from dialogue.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Dude, you think I live with my parents?

dialogue.speaker: Dave
dialogue.line: Oh, is that your dad, though?

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: No. That's just some old, crotchety Indian couple that I sublet to.

jokes.text: Bret discovers Doug in the basement of Mel's building. Doug insists he's 'happy down here' and asks Bret not to mention he's there.
jokes.setup: Bret goes to the toilet and encounters Mel's husband hiding in a basement.
jokes.punchline: Doug is voluntarily living in a basement to avoid Mel's dinner party — or possibly just in general — and is content there. He has work to do. 'Don't mention that you saw me here.'
jokes.explanation: The joke deepens Mel's portrait — her husband has retreated to a basement. 'Besides, I'm... I'm happy down here' is perfectly pathetic. The implication that this is Doug's regular arrangement, not just tonight, is the dark undertone that elevates it. Good payoff for Mel's restraining order monologue.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: The weird thing is they look like me. They've got photos of themselves with you as a kid.

dialogue.speaker: Dave
dialogue.line: I know. It's creepy. I think they make them on the computer.

jokes.text: Bret! Hey, Bret, can I have a quick word with you? Those girls, I don't trust them, Bret. They have no interest in you as musicians. — Just want to what? — They want to do it with you, Bret. — Sex? — Yes.
jokes.setup: Mel intercepts Bret on his way back from the bathroom/Doug area.
jokes.punchline: Mel warns Bret that the new fans want to have sex with him, framing this as a threat — and confirms it deadpan when Bret asks 'Sex?' The comedy is in Mel's protective jealousy masquerading as concern.
jokes.explanation: Mel's warning is driven by jealousy she frames as protective management. The 'Sex? — Yes.' confirmation exchange is a well-timed deadpan that underlines the absurdity. Bret's question ('Sex?') implies he needs it spelled out further, which is also character-accurate.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: That's... that's our bedroom.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Yep. That's a live webcam from your bedroom.

jokes.text: It's just, Bret, I just hope that you'll make sure that they respect your boundaries. — Thank you, Mel. Okay. — Yeah. — Okay. — Are you okay? Do you need any help with... — Uh, can you please leave? — Oh, you don't need any help? — I'm going to the toilet, yeah. — Everything's fine with you?
jokes.setup: Mel has cornered Bret in or near the bathroom.
jokes.punchline: Mel follows Bret to the actual toilet area and offers to 'help' — the cringe reaching its peak as she remains physically present through multiple polite dismissals.
jokes.explanation: This is sustained cringe at its best — Mel offering bathroom assistance escalates each beat. 'Everything's fine with you?' as a parting question suggests she's lingering at the door. The comedy is entirely in how wrong the situation is and how long it continues.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: I've had a really good response from something that happened with you when you were alone with the webcam. Do you know what that would be?

jokes.text: Jemaine, Mel thinks they're not interested in the band, they're interested in sex. — Sorry we have to run off, Mel. — That was delicious! Congratulations on the prize.
jokes.setup: Bret reports Mel's warning to Jemaine — that the new fans want sex.
jokes.punchline: This information, intended as a warning, causes them to immediately abandon Mel's dinner and flee to the new fans.
jokes.explanation: The irony that Mel's warning accelerates their departure toward the very thing she's warning against is perfectly structured. 'Congratulations on the prize' shouted as they leave — acknowledging the dinner competition win while abandoning it — is a nice comedic detail.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: I didn't know we had a webcam.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Okay, well, whatever you're doing, just be careful, all right?

jokes.text: So, uh, you guys want to get whacked off? — What? — Yeah. Whacked off, you know, do some drugs. — Mmm. — Oh.
jokes.setup: The guys meet Summer and Rain at a new location.
jokes.punchline: Summer uses 'whacked off' as a drug invitation, having apparently adopted the term from the guys' earlier usage — which had a completely different meaning.
jokes.explanation: The callback works cleanly — Summer has taken their fake drug slang at face value and is now using it herself. The guys' response 'What?' mirrors the girls' reaction to the term earlier, completing the circle. Good structural callback.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: We've tripled the fan base. So we need two new t-shirts made up.

jokes.text: Bret and Jemaine's excuses not to take acid: Bret has a cold (airborne), Jemaine has the same airborne cold, then they negotiate down to a 16th of a portion.
jokes.setup: Summer offers Bret and Jemaine acid. They want to decline but don't want to seem uncool.
jokes.punchline: The cold excuse is transparently invented, then Jemaine immediately claims the same airborne cold. Summer offers a half portion. They negotiate to halves of halves until Bret requests 'a 16th.' Summer gives him what she calls a 16th and then corrects herself — it's a piece of dry skin he dropped.
jokes.explanation: Multi-stage escalating joke with a perfect final beat. The negotiation structure (half → half of half → 16th) is mathematically absurd and perfectly Bret. The 'piece of dry skin' payoff — Summer corrects herself casually after giving it to him — is a sudden gross-out that lands with great impact. The 'Lick it!' command seals it. This sequence earns an 8 for the quality of its escalating structure and the specificity of the dry skin gag.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: You'll end up like ZZ Top.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: What are you talking about?

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: You know, ZZ Top.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Yeah?

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: They sang the song about the... the... the woman with the legs.

jokes.text: Oh, thank you. Is that it? — No, that's a dry piece of skin. You dropped it. — There you go. Go on. Lick it!
jokes.setup: Summer is handing Jemaine his 1/16th of the acid tab.
jokes.punchline: What Jemaine picks up isn't the drug — it's his own dry skin. Summer then hands him the actual piece and tells him to lick it.
jokes.explanation: The dry skin confusion makes a small drug transaction grotesque and funny simultaneously. 'Lick it!' as the closing command is both a drug instruction and inherently disgusting in context. Good physical comedy moment.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: they had a do-we-have-a-beard- or-not situation.

jokes.text: The 'Pretty Prince of Parties' song — surreal acid-trip musical number with nonsense rhymes ('flunky bunky dunky'), Mickey Maori minstrel, and sustained la-la section.
jokes.setup: The guys have taken acid (or possibly just a skin flake). A musical number begins representing the drug experience.
jokes.punchline: The song is deliberately unmoored, full of nonsense couplets, and eventually devolves into two minutes of la-la-la — the song has lost coherence, mirroring the acid trip.
jokes.explanation: The song is a formal joke — its musical form mirrors its content (a disintegrating mind). 'Flunky bunky dunky' and 'mickey Maori minstrel' function as nonsense that sounds like sense. The extended la-la section is the song doing to the listener what acid supposedly does to the brain — stranding them in a pleasant nowhere. 'Rama shalanka lanka Ravi Shankar' is a great piece of absurdist sound poetry.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: That's nice, like... kind of like bad weather.

jokes.text: Pretty Prince of Parties: 'You're a flunky bunky dunky' and 'I'm a pitcher of holy water'
jokes.setup: The song's psychedelic imagery involves the singers describing themselves and the women in surreal terms
jokes.punchline: 'Flunky bunky dunky' as a descriptor; 'I'm a pitcher of holy water' as a self-description that rhymes with 'You're a picture of the devil's daughter' — nonsense that follows internal logic
jokes.explanation: The rhyme scheme is tight ('picture of the devil's daughter / pitcher of holy water') while the imagery is maximally absurd. FotC songs always reward close reading of lyrics for these kinds of formal jokes.

dialogue.speaker: Mel
dialogue.line: Do you not have a computer, or...

dialogue.speaker: Rain
dialogue.line: No, I do have a computer.

dialogue.speaker: Mel
dialogue.line: Oh, you can't read.

jokes.text: Oh, pretty prince of parties / Where's the party now? — I don't know. / Oh, pretty prince of parties / Where does water go? — I let it flow.
jokes.setup: The acid trip song poses philosophical questions to the Pretty Prince of Parties.
jokes.punchline: The answers ('I don't know,' 'I let it flow') are sincere but also completely insufficient, and the questions themselves — 'Where does water go?' — are both profound to a stoned person and trivially knowable.
jokes.explanation: The call-and-response structure where profound questions receive mundane or evasive answers captures acid-trip logic perfectly. 'I can't come to your party — No' as a firm refusal to his own party is particularly funny.

dialogue.speaker: Rain
dialogue.line: Oh, I thought he was your dad.

dialogue.speaker: Mel
dialogue.line: He's a little bit older than me.

dialogue.speaker: Rain
dialogue.line: Yeah.

dialogue.speaker: Mel
dialogue.line: He was my college professor.

jokes.text: I'm the mickey Maori minstrel / You're the high priestess of tinsel / I'm the guru god of ganja / Rama shalanka lanka Ravi Shankar
jokes.setup: The acid trip song continues into a verse of competing identity claims.
jokes.punchline: 'Rama shalanka lanka Ravi Shankar' — the verse that begins with some kind of sense collapses into phoneme salad that rhymes with itself.
jokes.explanation: 'Mickey Maori minstrel' is a funny-sounding collision of alliterative references. The Ravi Shankar line works as a rhyme-forced piece of nonsense that sounds more meaningful than it is — which is exactly what acid reportedly does to meaning.

dialogue.speaker: Mel
dialogue.line: He resisted for a while and there were some legal... boundaries, you know, keeping me from being near him or... his family, but... in the end, love overcame.

jokes.text: I'm just gonna go to the toilet. I'll see you later.
jokes.setup: The acid song has ended with a long la-la section.
jokes.punchline: Bret's first words after the extended musical number are a casual toilet announcement and farewell — the mundane immediately following the surreal.
jokes.explanation: The comedic value is entirely in the register shift — from surreal musical number to 'I'm just gonna go to the toilet. I'll see you later.' The casualness is the joke. Also a callback to Bret's repeated toilet escape strategy.

dialogue.speaker: Mel
dialogue.line: I always get what I want.

jokes.text: I'm really kind of nervous. — How come? — I'm going to suggest that we have a threesome.
jokes.setup: Summer confesses nervousness to Jemaine in a quiet moment.
jokes.punchline: The source of nerves is a threesome proposition — not what was expected from a shy admission.
jokes.explanation: A functional misdirection — the vulnerability of nervousness delivers an unexpected payload. Works partly because Mel's warning set up the sex-interest subplot, making this a payoff.

dialogue.speaker: Mel
dialogue.line: But you don't drink beer, Bret.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: I drink it all the time.

dialogue.speaker: Mel
dialogue.line: I thought it always made you go to the toilet.

jokes.text: Oh. [pause] I just have to go and talk to Bret quickly.
jokes.setup: Summer proposes a threesome to Jemaine.
jokes.punchline: Jemaine's response is a beat of silence followed by needing to immediately consult Bret — his first instinct is managerial rather than romantic.
jokes.explanation: The comedy is entirely in what doesn't happen. A threesome proposition and Jemaine's response is to call a business meeting with Bret. The pause before 'I just have to go and talk to Bret quickly' is the whole joke. His relationship with Bret is apparently the relevant decision-making body for sexual invitations. Economy is near-perfect — maximum information in minimum words.

dialogue.speaker: Doug
dialogue.line: Oh, hey, Bret.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Are you gonna come up for dinner?

dialogue.speaker: Doug
dialogue.line: Uh, no. This is Mel's special day. Besides, I'm... I'm happy down here.

jokes.text: Bret, you got to go home. — Why? — Because I've been offered a threesome. — What about me? — No, you're not included. Go home.
jokes.setup: Jemaine has found Bret to tell him about the threesome offer.
jokes.punchline: Jemaine's plan is to send Bret home so he can have a twosome under the guise of a 'threesome' — Bret's indignant 'What about me?' and Jemaine's callous 'No, you're not included' is the exchange.
jokes.explanation: Bret's immediate 'What about me?' — taking the threesome as a band opportunity he has a right to be included in — is excellent character comedy. Both guys treat this as an administrative issue rather than a romantic situation.

dialogue.speaker: Mel
dialogue.line: Those girls, I don't trust them, Bret. They have no interest in you as musicians. They just want to...

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Just want to what?

dialogue.speaker: Mel
dialogue.line: They want to do it with you, Bret.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Sex?

jokes.text: This doesn't look like Prince.
jokes.setup: Bret is wearing what Dave gave him as 'Casual Prince.'
jokes.punchline: Bret himself acknowledges the blouse doesn't deliver on the Prince promise — a moment of fashion self-awareness delivered in total deadpan.
jokes.explanation: A clean callback to the Casual Prince bit. The comedy is in Bret standing in what is probably a woman's blouse having this realization while also trying to excuse himself from the dinner. Economy is the joke's strength.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: So, uh, you guys want to get whacked off?

dialogue.speaker: Summer
dialogue.line: What?

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Yeah. Whacked off, you know, do some drugs.

jokes.text: Bret's excuse: 'I have a doctor's appointment.' Fan: 'At night?' Bret: 'Yeah.' Then Summer asks Bret about the threesome.
jokes.setup: Bret needs to make an excuse to leave
jokes.punchline: His excuse is 'doctor's appointment at night' — which is immediately questioned and he just confirms it flatly; then Summer asks him specifically for the threesome
jokes.explanation: The bad excuse followed by flat confirmation is good. The twist that Summer then specifically invites Bret — after Jemaine spent the whole scene trying to exclude him — is a nice structural reversal.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: I have a cold.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: So I won't.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Yeah.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: I've also got the same cold. It's airborne.

jokes.text: Bret, do you want to have a threesome? — Oh. Just one second.
jokes.setup: Bret is making his excuses to leave the apartment.
jokes.punchline: Summer pivots to also offering Bret a threesome — confirming the offer is not Jemaine-specific. Bret's response is 'Just one second' — he needs to consult Jemaine.
jokes.explanation: The escalation that Bret is also being offered the threesome — making the whole Jemaine/Bret negotiation in the bathroom moot — is a strong structural joke. 'Just one second' mirrors Jemaine's bathroom consultation instinct exactly. Good symmetry.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Maybe just half my half again.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: I'll just have a 16th.

jokes.text: You ever had a threesome? — Nearly. — What you mean, nearly? — I had a twosome. — Wow. What was that like? — Great. I've done it several times, man. — Just one of you there, then one... — Yeah. — Oh no, I've had a twosome! — That's just the normal way. — Yes.
jokes.setup: Bret challenges Jemaine's claimed threesome expertise.
jokes.punchline: Jemaine's 'nearly' means he has had a 'twosome' — regular sex — which he treats as impressive until forced to acknowledge it's 'just the normal way.' Bret then realizes he has also had this achievement.
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's standout joke. The structure is almost perfectly executed: setup (challenging Jemaine's expertise), misdirection ('nearly had one'), deflation ('twosome'), escalation ('several times, man'), final twist (Bret realizes he also qualifies), and the button ('that's just the normal way'). Each beat earns the next. The comedy is character-specific — only these two characters could have this conversation this earnestly. Comparable to the 9.5 anchor in its structural precision and escalation quality, though the individual concept is slightly less striking. This is a genuine 8.5+ joke.

dialogue.line: No, that's a dry piece of skin. You dropped it.

jokes.text: Is it impolite not to do it considering she's offered and it's her place? — No. Can't do it. No. — I won't do it. — What, you're not doing it? — No way. — If you're not doing it, I might do it. — Are you gonna do it? — Yeah, why not? I'm gonna go for it. — Okay, let's do it. — No, don't do it.
jokes.setup: Back in the bathroom (again), the guys try to make a decision about the threesome.
jokes.punchline: The negotiation is an infinite loop: each refusal by one triggers willingness in the other, which triggers a reversal, which triggers another reversal.
jokes.explanation: The escalating loop structure where each position inverts the moment it's held is excellent sustained comedy. The politeness angle ('is it impolite not to do it?') as the opening frame is a great Jemaine entry point — he's approaching sexual decision-making as an etiquette problem.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Oh, flub!

jokes.text: Yeah, but if you do it and I'm not there, you're back to a two-way. — Oh, yes, you're right.
jokes.setup: Jemaine is considering doing it alone while Bret stays out.
jokes.punchline: Bret points out the math — without him, it's just a twosome, which Jemaine already nearly had. This is accepted as a decisive point.
jokes.explanation: The callback to the twosome logic is perfectly applied — Bret is using Jemaine's own framework against him. 'Oh, yes, you're right' as Jemaine's capitulation to the logic is excellent. The guys approaching sex as arithmetic is a sustained comedy engine in this section.

dialogue.line: Pretty Prince of Parties drug song with nonsensical lyrics

jokes.text: I think we should just go in there and say thank you and then go home. — No, we'll feel obliged to do it. — Let's just climb out the window and go home. Come on.
jokes.setup: The guys have decided they cannot do the threesome.
jokes.punchline: Rather than simply declining politely, they decide to climb out the window — escalating the social avoidance to extreme levels.
jokes.explanation: 'We'll feel obliged to do it' if they go back in is a great piece of character logic — their politeness makes rejection impossible face-to-face. Climbing out the window as the only exit strategy from social obligation is both absurd and completely character-true. The escalation from 'say thanks and go home' to 'climb out the window' in two lines is efficient comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: I'm going to suggest that we have a threesome.
Oh.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: I just have to go and talk to Bret quickly.

jokes.text: Oh, hi, Summer. — Hey. — Bret, what are you doing? — Um... yeah. — Um... — Oh, hey.
jokes.setup: The guys are attempting to climb out the window.
jokes.punchline: They are caught mid-escape by Summer — apparently the window leads to where she is. They are caught climbing out with no excuse.
jokes.explanation: The window plan failing because Summer is outside is good physical comedy. The extended 'Um... yeah... Um...' from both parties while Bret is presumably half out a window is pure cringe comedy. 'Oh, hey' from Jemaine as a second voice suggesting he's right behind Bret is a good implied visual.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: You got to go home.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Why?

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Because I've been offered a threesome.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: What about me?

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: No, you're not included.

jokes.text: Again, just want to say thank you for a delightful evening. — Thanks for the acids. — And the offer of the threesome. — And, um... — Really wonderful night.
jokes.setup: Having been caught climbing out the window, the guys pivot to formal farewells.
jokes.punchline: They thank Summer specifically for 'the acids' and 'the offer of the threesome' — treating these as dinner party amenities in the same category as the food.
jokes.explanation: Itemizing 'the acids' and 'the offer of the threesome' in formal thank-you format — as though these are equivalent to 'thank you for a lovely dinner and the wine' — is exceptional character comedy. The politeness register applied to drug use and sexual propositions is a perfect crystallization of the Conchords tone. 'Really wonderful night' as the sign-off is the perfect button. One of the episode's best jokes.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: You got it wrong. They want me in the threesome.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: What? That's not a threesome, that's a foursome.

jokes.text: The new fans gave us illegal drugs and wanted to have a threesome with us. — Whoa, really? — Mmm. — That's pretty rock 'n' roll. — No, it was very awkward.
jokes.setup: At the next band meeting, the guys report on what happened with the new fans.
jokes.punchline: Murray's evaluation of drugs and a threesome offer is 'that's pretty rock 'n' roll.' The guys counter that it was 'very awkward' — the diametrically opposed response.
jokes.explanation: The contrast between rock and roll mythology and the guys' actual experience is the show's central tension stated plainly. 'Very awkward' as the only counter to Murray's romance of rock life is both accurate and funny.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: You got it wrong. They want me in the threesome.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: What? That's not a threesome, that's a foursome.

jokes.text: Murray's band meeting response to 'the new fans gave us illegal drugs and wanted to have a threesome': 'Whoa, really? That's pretty rock 'n' roll.'
jokes.setup: The band reports the events of the night to Murray
jokes.punchline: Murray's response to their uncomfortable experience is impressed admiration — he views it as evidence they're finally making it
jokes.explanation: Murray's rock aspirations make him completely misread the situation. 'That's pretty rock 'n' roll' for an experience the band found deeply awkward is good character comedy. Sets up his subsequent rant about drugs and threesomes being standard practice.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: You ever had a threesome?

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Nearly.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: What you mean, nearly?

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: I had a twosome.

jokes.text: You'll be offered drugs. You'll have threesomes, dinners, you know? You'll end up going into town in a taxi, have a couple of drugs, have dinner, have a threesome, go home again. Have a shower, go out again, more drugs, more threesomes... happens all the time.
jokes.setup: Murray is explaining the rock star lifestyle to the guys.
jokes.punchline: Murray's rock star schedule is a repeated loop of taxi-drugs-dinner-threesome-shower with matter-of-fact escalation, ending in 'happens all the time' — which is possibly something he knows from managing other bands, or possibly completely fabricated.
jokes.explanation: The repeated cycle structure — taxi, drugs, dinner, threesome, shower, repeat — delivered in the same tone Murray uses for band meeting minutes, is very funny. 'Have a shower, go out again' as a mundane hygiene note within the hedonism list is the best detail. 'I'd do it' as his closing envy is a good button.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Yeah, I've done that. I've had a one-way and a two-way.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Oh no, I've had a twosome!

jokes.text: All right? I mean, I'd do it.
jokes.setup: Murray has just finished describing the rock star threesome-drugs lifestyle as normal and aspirational.
jokes.punchline: Murray's personal endorsement: 'I'd do it.' Delivered as a genuine aside.
jokes.explanation: Economy joke — three words that reveal Murray's personal relationship to the fantasy he's describing. His slight envy of the band's situation is humanizing and funny. The understatement is perfect: not 'I'd love that' but simply 'I'd do it.'

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Summer, where's Rain?

dialogue.speaker: Summer
dialogue.line: Oh, Rain went home. She didn't want to be here while we were all having a threesome.

jokes.text: Okay, now look, you've lost your fan list now. The two new fans have dropped out of the gang. — We still got Mel. — Nope, she's gone as well. She's off the list. — Really? — Yes. — How many fans have we got now? — None. Empty club. I've put a note here... no one.
jokes.setup: Murray does the fan list accounting after the evening's events.
jokes.punchline: The fan list has gone from hundreds (all Mel) to two new fans to zero — 'Empty club. I've put a note here... no one.'
jokes.explanation: 'Empty club. I've put a note here... no one.' is a great Murray line — he has formally documented their zero-fan status in the meeting notes. The careful record-keeping of their own failure is perfect character comedy. The escalation from hundreds → two → zero completes the episode's fan-count arc.

dialogue.line: Extended threesome negotiation in bathroom with both deciding not to participate

jokes.text: Murray asks Jemaine to join the fan list; Jemaine says yes. Murray asks Bret. Bret: 'No way. No, I'm just not a fan of the band at all.'
jokes.setup: With zero fans, Murray reopens the fan list
jokes.punchline: Jemaine rejoins (he was disqualified in the opening); Bret maintains throughout that he genuinely doesn't like the band — not as a bit, but sincerely
jokes.explanation: Beautiful full-circle callback. The episode ends exactly where it started: Jemaine as the band's only fan, Bret genuinely not being one. 'I'm just not a fan of the band at all' as the episode's closing statement is perfectly deadpan and thematically resonant. Justifies 8+ for structure and character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Are you doing it?

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: You're not doing it?

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: No.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Okay, I won't do it.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Well, if you're not doing it, then I think I'm... yeah, I'm gonna do it.

jokes.text: Oh no, here we go. Perhaps here, Jemaine, would you like to join the list? — Yes. — Yeah? Okay. What about you, Bret? Can you join the fan list? — No way. — No, I'm just not a fan of the band at all.
jokes.setup: The fan list is empty. Murray looks at Jemaine.
jokes.punchline: Jemaine, re-admitted as a fan after earlier disqualification, eagerly rejoins. Bret still refuses on principle — he remains 'not a fan of the band at all.'
jokes.explanation: The episode-closing callback to the opening fan list jokes is structurally satisfying. Jemaine's immediate 'Yes' to rejoining shows nothing has changed for him. Bret's 'No way... I'm just not a fan of the band at all' echoes his opening line about the Bee Gees and Pearl Jam, completing the character arc (or non-arc) perfectly.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Let's just climb out the window and go home. Come on.

jokes.text: Hey, Jemaine. — Hmm? — Last night, did you... look? — We agreed never to talk about this. — Yeah, but did you look? — I didn't look. — Did you look? — I had my eyes shut most of the time. — What do you mean most of the time? — I opened them a little bit so I could see what I was doing.
jokes.setup: In bed after the meeting, Bret raises the unresolved question of what actually happened the previous night.
jokes.punchline: Jemaine insists he didn't look, then qualifies that he had his eyes shut 'most of the time,' then explains he opened them 'a little bit so I could see what I was doing' — confirming that something did happen and that he was participatory.
jokes.explanation: The best joke in the episode. The escalating qualifications — 'didn't look' → 'most of the time' → 'so I could see what I was doing' — tell a complete story through what's not said. Each retreat from the denial position confirms more than it denies. 'We agreed never to talk about this' before the conversation has barely started is excellent. The joke requires the entire episode's setup to land fully. Structurally this is as good as anything in Conchords — the denial-escalation structure rivals the vasectomy anchor example in precision, just with smaller cultural footprint.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: What, for the manager to e-mail the fans and offer them man-man-lady threesome?

jokes.text: Actually, me and her both took a break for a while. You were just up there by yourself.
jokes.setup: Bret has asked what Jemaine was doing with a chair during the alleged threesome.
jokes.punchline: Jemaine reveals that both he and Summer took a break — leaving Bret alone, apparently continuing.
jokes.explanation: A masterful escalation of an already excellent bit. The implication that the threesome had intermissions, that Jemaine and Summer both needed breaks, and that Bret kept going alone — is both absurd and completely plausible given the characters. 'You were just up there by yourself' is perfectly deadpan. The joke escalates the previous joke (81) in a way that makes both jokes better. Together these closing jokes form the episode's comedic peak.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: You'll end up going into town in a taxi, have a couple of drugs, have dinner, have a threesome, go home again. Have a shower, go out again, more drugs, more threesomes... happens all the time.

jokes.text: Murray's voice from webcam: 'Go to sleep. Stop chatting.' / 'All right, Murray.' / 'Good night.' / 'Say good night for me.' / 'Just wave at him.' / 'Say goodnight to Mel.' / 'All right. Good night, Mel.' / Mel's voice: 'Good night.'
jokes.setup: The episode closes with Bret and Jemaine in bed
jokes.punchline: Murray is watching via webcam and tells them to go to sleep; then Mel is also apparently watching — the entire apartment ecosystem of surveillance closes the episode
jokes.explanation: Perfect closing callback to the webcam subplot. Murray and Mel monitoring them like parents via webcam is a great final image for an episode about the band failing to achieve adult rock-star independence. 'Just wave at him' / 'Say goodnight to Mel' — the domesticity of it all — is a beautiful episode button. The show's thesis (they can't escape their weird family) stated wordlessly.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: How many fans have we got now?

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: None. Empty club. I've put a note here... no one.

tmdb_overview: A performance at a local "World Music Jam" results in the Conchords gaining two new fans, but Mel is suspicious of the newcomers and their motives. Murray encourages the band to adopt a more "rock star" attitude.
